output:
I'd play Kd6. Your move is a good, calm king move: it brings the king toward the center and keeps the position under control. After Ke4, the simple plan is Kc6, staying active rather than rushing the pawn. In rook-and-pawn endings, improving the king first is often the safest way to avoid unnecessary counterplay.

Takeaway: In endgames, activate your king before pushing pawns unless there is a clear forcing reason.

I'd play b2+. Your Kd6 was fine, but b2+ is the more forcing choice: it moves the pawn with check, so White has to respond before choosing their own plan. If White goes Ke4, then Ra4+ keeps Black active and makes White keep solving problems. Takeaway: In rook endings, look first for forcing pawn moves with check before making a quiet king move.

I'd play Kd6. Your move is a strong, calm choice: it improves the king without forcing anything prematurely. After Kd6, if White goes Ke4, Black can continue Kc6, keeping the position under control and making steady progress with the king. This is exactly the kind of practical endgame move advanced players should trust.

Takeaway: In rook endings, quiet king improvement is often just as important as forcing moves.

I'd play Be6. Your f4 move is playable, but Be6 is a cleaner beginner move because it improves the bishop while defending the pawn on g4. In this kind of endgame, before pushing pawns, look for a move that protects your own pawns and keeps your bishop active.  
Takeaway: In endgames, improve a piece and defend a pawn before making extra pawn moves.

I'd play Be6. This improves your bishop while also defending the pawn on g4, so White keeps the kingside structure steady instead of just pushing another pawn with f4. A simple plan is to stay flexible: if Black plays Ba5, you can bring the bishop back to f5 and keep the position under control.  
Takeaway: In quiet endings, prefer moves that improve a piece and protect a weakness at the same time.

I'd play Kd1. Your f4 was playable, but Kd1 is a cleaner endgame move: improve the king while attacking the bishop on d2. If Black answers with Ba5, you can calmly return with Ke2, having kept everything under control without committing another pawn.  
Takeaway: In simplified positions, look first for king moves that gain activity with tempo.

I'd play Rxg5. Your move is a good, practical choice: you take the undefended pawn on g5, while your rook also attacks the pawn on g2 and defends your pawn on g7. For a beginner, this is exactly the right habit in rook endgames: remove loose pawns and keep your own pawns protected.  
Takeaway: When you can safely win an undefended pawn while improving your piece, do it.

I'd play h5. Your Rxg5 was perfectly playable, but h5 is a nice advanced endgame move because it first challenges the undefended g5 pawn without committing the rook. After h5, if Kh3, then Rxg5 wins that pawn while keeping the structure under control. This is the kind of useful patience that often improves rook endgames: make the opponent respond before choosing the final capture.

Takeaway: When a pawn is loose, look for a forcing pawn move that improves your position before grabbing it.

I'd play Rxg5. Your move is a good one: the pawn on g5 is undefended, so Black should simply take it. Even better, the rook then attacks the pawn on g2 while also defending the pawn on g7, so one move solves defense and creates pressure.  
Takeaway: In rook endgames, grab loose pawns when the capture also improves your rook’s activity.

I'd play Nf2. Your move was a good practical choice: the knight attacks the rook on h1 and the pawn on h3 at the same time. For a beginner, that is exactly the kind of active move to look for—use your pieces to create direct problems instead of just waiting.  
Takeaway: When you can improve a piece while attacking something, that is usually a strong candidate move.

I'd play Rh8. Your h6 was playable, but Rh8 is a calmer beginner-friendly choice because it first defends the pawn on h5 before you decide whether to push it. In rook endgames, it’s often useful to improve your rook and make your pawns safer before moving them forward.

Takeaway: Before pushing a pawn, ask whether you can first make it better protected.

I'd play Ra7. Your h6 was playable, but Ra7 is more instructive because it uses the rook actively and attacks the pawn on a5 right away. The concrete plan is to keep creating pressure with the rook before deciding which pawn to push next.  
Takeaway: In rook endings, look for active rook moves that attack a target before making a pawn move.

I'd play c3. Your h6 was playable, so this is more about move order than a real mistake. c3 first is a calm improving move, and if Black replies Rh4, you can still continue with h6. This keeps your useful pawn push in reserve while making progress elsewhere first.  
Takeaway: When your active idea will still be available next move, look for a quiet improvement before committing.

I'd play Qd3. Your c4 is playable, but Qd3 is the cleaner improving move: it defends both b3 and c2 while keeping your queen active. A simple plan is Qd3, and if Black responds with Re6, then c4 can come next with your queen and pawns better coordinated.  
Takeaway: Before pushing a pawn, look for a queen move that improves activity while defending key pawns.

Takeaway: When nothing is urgent, choose a developing move that also protects something important.
